Your accounts all show $0 balances today — all five were connected manually just today (June 5), so no live balance data has synced yet. This means I can't directly assess whether you're holding too much or too little cash right now.
That said, I can give you a really useful framework based on what I do know about your situation.
The right cash cushion for a freelancer
As a self-employed UX consultant with uneven income, your cash reserve needs are genuinely higher than a salaried employee's. There are actually two separate cash "buckets" worth thinking about:
Emergency fund (personal) The standard 3–6 months of essential living expenses applies here, but since you're a one-income household with no employer safety net, leaning toward 6 months is the smarter call. Based on your May spending, your core personal expenses look like roughly $2,685/month (rent $2,100, groceries $365, healthcare $575 — noting healthcare is a big one without employer coverage, and therapy adds to that). A 6-month cushion would be around $16,000–$17,000 in an accessible account.
Tax reserve (business) This is the one freelancers most often undersize. You paid $4,200 in estimated taxes in April, and your May income alone was $13,960 across three clients. The IRS expects quarterly payments, and Texas has no state income tax (a nice advantage). A rough rule of thumb is to set aside 25–30% of every client payment the moment it lands. Your Ally Tax Reserve Savings account is perfectly named for exactly this purpose — keeping it separate is the right instinct.
The idle cash question
Once you have those two buckets funded, additional cash sitting in a low-yield checking account would genuinely be idle. Your Bluevine Business Checking is worth a look here — Bluevine has historically offered competitive interest on business checking balances, so that cash may already be working for you. For personal savings beyond your emergency fund, a high-yield savings account (Origin offers one, and I'd also encourage comparing rates at places like Marcus or Ally) could meaningfully outpace a standard checking account.
A suggested structure to consider
Operating buffer (1–2 months of business expenses): in your Bluevine business checking — covers the gap between invoicing and payment
Tax reserve (25–30% of gross income, ongoing): in your Ally Tax Reserve Savings, kept strictly separate
Personal emergency fund (~$16,000–$17,000): in a high-yield savings account
Anything above those three buckets: worth putting to work in short-term investments or a brokerage account
The uneven income isn't a reason to hold all cash — it's a reason to hold structured cash with clear purpose for each dollar.
